payload.text:
Hello, I'm fairly technically adept but not in website making. So it would be good if you explain what you're doing and make the code easy to modify. I need a static site for my brand, Goblin Grafix. I'm a creator, so I want a fancy 3js masthead that says "GOBLIN GRAFIX" in 3D text and has some chromatic aberration shader. We'll replace that with my own 3d file later. Then we should have big links for patreon, youtube, instagram, and tiktok. Then there should be a business email. Then there should be a form to put in your email for a newsletter. It should overall look like neocities, in terms of "vibe".
The font should be courier new. It should look barebones except for the masthead. The buttons to click on should be big, square, and with the logo for the app there. Here are the links.

https://www.youtube.com/@goblingrafix
https://www.patreon.com/GoblinGrafix
https://www.instagram.com/goblingrafix/
https://www.tiktok.com/@goblingrafix

Make the masthead font courier new bold. Make it formatted like

"GOBLIN
GRAFIX"

with one word on top of another. Add a light grey stroke to the text. The text should be gently wobbling.

**Envisioning the Design**

I'm now zeroing in on the aesthetic for "Goblin Grafix." It needs to be raw, pixelated, and a little bit glitchy, yet sophisticated. I'm imagining a Three.js masthead with a retro-futuristic flair. This feels like the right direction. The challenge now is to balance the "barebones" feel with a professional presentation.


**Refining Technical Specs**

I've firmed up the technical direction. React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS form the foundation. For 3D elements, I'm leaning heavily on React Three Fiber and Drei. Lucide React should provide plenty of standard icons, but I might need to source specific brand icons for social media. The XML output requirement is clear, and the overall structure is coming into focus.


**Revising Icon Strategy**

I've revised the icon approach. Initially, I thought `react-icons` might be necessary, but I'm now leaning towards inline SVG components for Patreon and TikTok logos. This keeps things lean and consistent with the "barebones" feel. For standard UI, I'll stick with `lucide-react`, and use generic Lucide icons where possible.


**Constructing Core Components**

I'm now building out the core React components. The file structure is becoming clear: `index.tsx`, `App.tsx`, the `components` directory for reusable parts, including `Masthead.tsx`, `SocialButton.tsx`, `NewsletterForm.tsx`, and `Layout.tsx` for that "barebones" feel. I'm focusing now on the visual elements of the Masthead.


**Optimizing Text Rendering**

I'm now zeroing in on the text rendering strategy.  I'm exploring options for achieving a high-quality "Courier"-style font within the 3D Masthead. Concerns about external font JSON availability have led me to `@react-three/drei`'s `Text3D`, focusing on finding a reliable CDN for a monospace font. Shader effects, specifically chromatic aberration, are on the agenda via `@react-three/postprocessing` as a post-processing effect.


**Prioritizing Text Design**

I've decided to use `@react-three/drei`'s `Text3D` for a 3D feel, loading a generic font URL. The goal is a clean, robust solution. I was initially worried about finding a reliable CORS-friendly Courier font, but `Text` wasn't the right choice to keep this "3D" and professional. I'll utilize `EffectComposer` for post-processing effects, starting with chromatic aberration.


**Refining Visual Style**

I'm solidifying the visual approach. The background will be black with white text in Courier New. The layout will center, but maintain a raw aesthetic. Button design involves large squares with simple hover effects. I'll create SVG components for the social media logos. This will create a specific Neocities-inspired barebones feel.



**Adding User Feedback**

I'm now integrating feedback. I'll add descriptive comments for transparency and use clear variable names for easy modification. This will be the main approach to implementing the user requests. I've got to ensure the code remains functional. I'm focusing on the SVG paths for the branding icons. I've also thought about refining the Three.js code with a "wobble" effect and chromatic aberration. I want to stack the text for a better feel.


**Adapting to Feedback**

I'm now revisiting the text rendering strategy. Initially, I was going to use `@react-three/drei`'s `Text`, but, based on previous prompt feedback, `Text3D` is the right choice. I will try to use a standard Three.js font and will comment within the code on how to swap it out for a Courier font, so the user can modify later. The text will not have a "depth" (as such), but will be flat with a perspective camera. I am confident this solves the problem for now.


**Planning the Structure**

The project structure is now finalized, with clear files for the main app, Three.js masthead, social links, and SVG icons. Focusing now on `ThreeMasthead`, I'm planning the Three.js scene: canvas, lights for text, and post-processing for a chromatic aberration effect. `useFrame` will animate the slight rotation. For the font, I'll default to the reliable `helvetiker` from Three.js examples, adding a comment on how to use a custom font like Courier New.


**Updating Font Approach**

I've revised the text strategy. Initially, I planned for flat text via `Text`, but to satisfy the requirement for "3D text," I'm now opting for `Text3D` using the default Helvetiker font. I will style the remainder of the site in Courier New in HTML. I'll include a clear comment in the code to swap the font file later, making it easier for the end-user to upload a Courier font file if they want to.


**Optimizing Font Integration**

I'm now revising my text integration strategy again. Previously, I was planning to use the Three.js default fonts, with a note for later adjustment. Now, to perfectly satisfy the "Courier New" request, I'm going to leverage `drei`'s `Text` (SDF) component and load Courier Prime directly from Google Fonts, which seems an ideal solution. This allows full control over the font, without needing manual files.


**Completing Text3D Implementation**

I'm now fully committed to the `Text3D` component for the masthead text, using `helvetiker_bold` from the Three.js examples. The goal is to provide a solid base for the user to replace with their own font file later. I'll thoroughly document within the code how to do this, using the `font` prop. Dependencies are now correctly added.


**Finalizing Core Elements**

I'm solidifying the final pieces. I'm prioritizing the generic SVGs for brand icons and the helper component.  I've added a basic wobble effect to the masthead text, using `useFrame`, and applied the "Courier New" font via CSS to the main container, as planned. The core is now complete.

TypeScript definitions for the project.

A simple, brutalist newsletter signup form.
The main layout assembly.

diffs.replacement:
name: Deploy to GitHub Pages

on:
  push:
    branches: [ "main", "master" ]
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

concurrency:
  group: "pages"
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"
          cache: 'npm'
          
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm install
        
      - name: Build
        run: npm run build
        
      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: ./dist

  deploy:
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: build
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
        id: deployment
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

I'll include the necessary `package.json`, `tsconfig.json`, and ensure that they can easily verify deployment progress by pointing to the "Actions" tab. My goal is to streamline the process, avoiding unnecessary complexity and ensuring they can swiftly get their project online.

The reason your GitHub Pages settings look like that (with the default templates) is because the "worker" (GitHub Action) that builds your site **failed** or hasn't run successfully yet.

The main reason it likely failed is that we moved from a "no-build" setup to a "build" setup, but we forgot to give the builder the list of ingredients it needs (React, Three.js, etc.). In the coding world, this ingredient list is called **`package.json`**. Without it, the builder doesn't know what to install.

Here are the files you need to add to fix the build.

**What to do:**
1.  **Add/Update these files** in your repository.
2.  **Push** the changes to GitHub.
3.  **Wait about 2-3 minutes**.
4.  Click the **"Actions"** tab (next to "Pull requests") in your GitHub repo. You should see a workflow running. Once it turns green, the "Settings > Pages" tab will automatically update to show your live site.

**Investigating JSX Override**

I've pinpointed the root cause! It appears a global declaration is overriding `JSX.IntrinsicElements` incorrectly, leading to unrecognized standard HTML elements. Analyzing errors further, `types.ts` lacked a `React` import. Additionally, `components/Icons.tsx` and `components/Masthead3D.tsx` revealed the `JSX.IntrinsicElements` override, which omitted the standard HTML/SVG tags, confirming my suspicion.


**Analyzing Type Augmentation**

I've been thinking about the implications of the direct `JSX.IntrinsicElements` override. It's becoming clear that this approach is fundamentally flawed because it obliterates standard HTML elements, which is the cause of most type errors. I need to augment the existing types, or rely on `@react-three/fiber` for its type handling. Removing the override seems like the best initial step. However, using the correct module augmentation method or the `extend` function will fix any potential Three.js element gaps.


**Addressing Type Gaps**

My current thinking revolves around augmenting the `JSX.IntrinsicElements` interface rather than replacing it to correctly address R3F component typing without removing standard HTML tags. I'm leaning toward using the `@react-three/fiber` module augmentation method for cleaner and more robust type handling.


**Resolving Type Merging**

I'm now considering the best method to combine our Three.js components with standard HTML elements in the `JSX.IntrinsicElements` interface. It appears the current implementation is replacing, not merging. I need to figure out how to augment `@types/react`'s `IntrinsicElements` effectively, and avoid replacing the original definition. It appears TypeScript interface merging is key here. I need to make sure the project setup is correctly using `@types/react`, and augment the existing types appropriately.


**Prioritizing Code Simplification**

My top priority right now is removing the problematic `declare global` block from `Masthead3D.tsx`. I believe this will fix the type conflicts by allowing standard HTML elements like `<div>` to work again. I'm hoping that `@react-three/fiber` will correctly type the R3F elements, making this manual override unnecessary. If needed, I might consider preserving existing keys. The goal is to avoid global app-breaking issues in favor of specific library concerns.


**Debugging State Type Inference**

I'm now focusing on how the `state` type within the `ErrorBoundary` component isn't being properly inferred by TypeScript. I've realized that explicitly declaring `state: { hasError: boolean } = { hasError: false };` within the class body could fix the issues, potentially resolving the type errors. This is in contrast to the constructor-based initialization that's causing the inference problems. I will try this out now.
